dc.abstract.en.value: Education about Auschwitz and the Holocaust as a sphere of practical activity and a subject matter of pedagogical reflection establishes various objectives and effects for itself. They concern the knowledge of facts and formation of attitudes and values. This education concerns essentially the youth, but also other categories of recipients. It is conducted under the changing cultural, societal, and political circumstances. Education about Auschwitz and the Holocaust, in particular its objectives, effects, and conditions, has also become a subject matter of empirical research conducted by representatives of various disciplines of the social sciences, in particular psychology and sociology, and by educators themselves. One of the leading problems taken both in the pedagogical reflection and educational practice as well as in empirical research is the question about the quality and effectiveness of teaching about Auschwitz and the Holocaust as a means of formation of attitudes of the youth through the transfer of knowledge. In this article, we discuss this problematics based upon the results and methodological assumptions of the most important social research projects concerning education about Auschwitz and the Holocaust that have been carried out in Poland since the 1990s. We also consider what conclusions for the educational practice one may draw from this research  and what research approaches and means of measurement seem to best diagnose education about Auschwitz and the Holocaust as far as its objectives, effects, and conditions are concerned.

dc.abstract.pl.value: Edukacja o Auschwitz i Holokauście, jako sfera działań praktycznych i przedmiot refleksji pedagogiki, stawia sobie różne cele i zakłada rozmaite efekty, dotyczące znajomości faktów oraz kształtowania postaw i wartości. Obejmuje zasadniczo młodzież, ale i inne kategorie odbiorców. Prowadzona jest w zmieniających się warunkach kulturowych, społecznych i politycznych. Edukacja o Auschwitz i Holokauście, w tym szczególnie problematyka jej celów, efektów i uwarunkowań, stała się również przedmiotem badań empirycznych prowadzonych przez przedstawicieli różnych dyscyplin nauk społecznych, zwłaszcza psychologii i socjologii, oraz samych edukatorów. Jednym z wiodących problemów podejmowanym zarówno w refleksji pedagogicznej i praktyce edukacyjnej, jak i w badaniach empirycznych jest pytanie o jakość i skuteczność nauczania o Auschwitz i Zagładzie jako sposobu kształtowania postaw młodzieży poprzez przekazywanie wiedzy. W niniejszym artykule podejmujemy dyskusję wokół tej problematyki w oparciu o przedstawione przez nas wyniki i założenia metodologiczne najważniejszych spośród badań społecznych dotyczących edukacji o Auschwitz i Holokauście zrealizowanych w Polsce od lat 1990. Zastanowimy się też, jakie wnioski dla praktyki edukacyjnej płyną z tych badań oraz jakie podejście badawcze i sposoby pomiaru zdają się najlepiej diagnozować edukację o Auschwitz i Holokauście pod kątem jej celów, efektów i uwarunkowań.

Tymi słowami Robert K. Merton opisał rozprawę doktorską, którą pisałem w latach 1996-1999 na Uniwersytecie Mikołaja Kopernika w Toruniu pod kierunkiem Lecha Witkowskiego, wybitny filozof i pedagog, którego praca i osiągnięcia mają trwały wpływ na rozwój nauki w Polsce i na świecie. Właściwie trzeba podkreślić, że jego wszechstronność, interdyscyplinarne podejście oraz głęboka pasja do wiedzy czynią go wyjątkową postacią w dziedzinie filozofii i edukacji. Ponieważ co zauważył Merton jest to niezwykłe połączenie czynników, ciekawym może okazać się, jak zrodził się pomysł napisania doktoratu w Polsce pod kierunkiem Witkowskiego.

dc.abstract.en.value: To build sustainable cities and communities (SDG Goal 11), all sections of society, especially marginalized groups, need to be included in the development processes. As the global population ages, the importance of creating inclusive cities is becoming increasingly evident. By prioritizing the needs and preferences of older adults, cities may foster conditions that support independent living, social interaction, and healthy ageing. Urban planning is essential in this regard since it guarantees that the requirements and preferences of older adults are taken into consideration when designing cities. Recognizing older adults’ needs in urban environments can be achieved by involving them in planning and decision-making processes. It is very important to ensure the active participation of them in determining the priority of age-friendly urban planning strategies. For this purpose, geodata collection tools need to be developed and adapted to the needs of older adults. Geodata was gathered for this study using KoBo Toolbox, a free web-based, open-source data collecting tool. This tool facilitates data collection through surveys conducted in the field using mobile devices like smartphones and tablets. The study reveals the preliminary findings of data collected via KoBo Toolbox from four European cities (Bucharest, Kraków, The Hague, and Wrocław) regarding domains acknowledged within the WHO Age-Friendly Cities Framework. The findings also demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of the KoBo Toolbox in facilitating inclusive data collection for age-friendly urban planning initiatives. By incorporating the perspectives of older adults through participatory data collection methods, cities can develop and implement strategies that truly meet the needs of their ageing populations and provide inclusive, sustainable, and liveable urban environments for all.

dc.title.value: Engaging older adults in urban planning in four European cities : fostering inclusive and sustainable cities

dc.abstract.en.value: Crises caused by the COVID-19 pandemic lasted much longer than many people previously anticipated, with new waves of coronavirus leading to repeated lockdowns of the economy. Therefore, many governments have decided to help enterprises survive this hard time, particularly small- and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), which do not have as many sources of financing as large firms. However, there are differences in aid, both in terms of scale and tools, between countries, even if they belong to the European Union (EU). The aim of the paper is to compare support schemes for SMEs in Greece and Poland during the COVID-19 pandemic. In order to describe the effects of the schemes, the article juxtaposes publicly available data on unemployment and evolution of SMEs. The results show that unemployment was controlled in both countries, while the balance between newly created companies and companies that closed during the pandemic was not negative. A significant difference appeared in the evolution of deposits, most likely because of contrast in taking advantage of the Recovery and Resilience Fund. The paper contributes to widening the knowledge of economic crises and public policy, and the results could be interesting for policymakers.

dc.title.value: Support for SMEs in Greece and Poland during COVID-19

dc.abstract.en.value: Unemployment is extremely low in all countries of the Visegrad Group (V4) in recent years. The author presents changes of the V4 countries unemployment rates in the last fifteen years after accession to the European Union. The differences between men and women unemployment  and changes in long-term unemployment were also shown. In terms of employment growth positive changes have occurred in all V4 countries since 2004. In addition, there has been an increase in the countries’ productivity. Despite the all positive changes during last fifteen years, the significant differences between the V4 countries persist  e.g. the best situation is invariably on the Czech labour market, on the opposite side is Slovakia and Poland.

dc.abstract.en.value: Sociology has had an intimate relationship with crisis since its inception as a "positive science". It was established amidst the turbulence accompanying the emergence of modern industrial society in Western Europe, which appeared within the existing cognitive framework as an incomprehensible catastrophe. The newborn sociology, modeled after the triumphant natural sciences, immediately triggered an epistemological crisis that persists to this day. Despite the lack of a universally recognized paradigm, the sociological mainstream has generally accepted the model of "modernity" as a universal formation, co-created by the free market and liberal democracy under the aegis of the nation-state. This model now seems to be crumbling before our eyes, which may mean we are in a situation much like the 19th century: trapped within a specific cognitive framework, unable to perceive the new forms of social life emerging. Or, even worse, the dominant model of modernity was used as a colonizing tool, contributing to the current crisis. This presents a significant theoretical challenge, demanding that we go beyond what once seemed "obvious" to explore alternative epistemologies and unknown territories. In this book, we argue that Central-Eastern Europe offers a particularly valuable case study for the social sciences. First, its colonial experience is significantly more complex, as it faced the imperial ambitions of not only Western Europe but also Russia and the Ottoman Empire (e.g., Bulgaria). Second, socialism represented a clear attempt to escape Western domination and to create an alternative model of socio-economic development. Third, CEE has survived the "programming" of society twice, as capitalism was reinstalled in the early 1990s in a similarly top-down manner. These natural, large-scale social experiments can help us move beyond preconceived notions of what is "normal" or "modern" and instead explore alternative approaches to theorizing and researching society.

dc.abstract.en.value: The growing interest in the problems of evaluation among researchers and reformers Polish social life seems to indicate not only that soon it will be entered into the landscape of phenomena and processes in social reality, but also will constitute one of the essential components of this reality. Bearing in mind the perspective of the development of various projects in the area of evaluation as well as strengthening its position in the many disciplines and social sciences, not without significance is the fact that the area has a wealth of theoretical bases, approaches and strategies, and their broad application possibilities. The aim of the article is to analyze the basic assumptions of social evaluation.

Celem artykułu jest analiza podstawowych założeń ewaluacji w pomocy społecznej.

dc.abstract.en.value: This article’s objective is to analyse the influence of international anti-corruption pressure on national measures aimed at combating corruption in Poland. First, the change in perception of corruption in Poland will be addressed, along with a short discussion of attempts to reduce its scope. Second, the anti-corruption measures of international organisations, such as the United Nations, the OECD, the Council of Europe, GRECO and the European Union, as well as international Conventions adopted and ratified by Poland will be examined. Third, the role of state and non-governmental institutions involved in the fight against corruption in Poland will be reviewed. Finally, the process of implementing governmental anti-corruption strategies will be analysed, with a particular focus on the introduction of legislative changes which are aimed at limiting corruption through the processes of legal modernisation and harmonisation. This paper argues that from among the many initiatives undertaken by various international institutions combating corruption in Poland, the most effective was found to be pressure by the European Union. Poland’s eagerness to enter the EU, and to make use of structural funds after accession, forced the country’s elected governments to implement certain recommendations of the European Commission regarding anti-corruption policy. However, these activities had, to a certain degree, a façade like character and were addressed to fulfill the formal requirements of the European Commission instead of serving as a genuine effort to curb corruption. Even though corruption has been officially considered a serious social problem, which needs to be combated and many anti-corruption measures have been implemented, the effects of the actions undertaken by consecutive Polish governments are not fully satisfactory. Also, anti-corruption slogans have been instrumentally used to fight against political opponents. From another perspective, the activities of Polish non-governmental organisations have contributed to a visible reduction in social tolerance for corruption. Although public opinion research shows that the level of corruption is slowly decreasing in Poland and the country’s position in the Corruption Perception Index is systematically improving, it is uncertain how strongly rooted this positive tendency will find itself in the long-term.
